content.text:
Two-row notation, while complete, is cumbersome. A more elegant and 
efficient representation is **cycle notation**, which captures the 
orbit structure of a permutation.

content.title: Cycle
content.content:
A permutation $\sigma \in S_n$ is called a **$k$-cycle** 
(or cycle of length $k$) if there exist distinct elements $a_1, a_2, 
\ldots, a_k$ such that:
\[
 \sigma(a_1) = a_2, \quad \sigma(a_2) = a_3, \quad \ldots, \quad 
 \sigma(a_{k-1}) = a_k, \quad \sigma(a_k) = a_1
\]
and $\sigma$ fixes all other elements. We write this as 
$(a_1\ a_2\ \cdots\ a_k)$.

content.title: Cycles in $S_5$
content.problem:
- $(1\ 3\ 5)$ is a $3$-cycle: $1 \to 3 \to 5 \to 1$, and $2, 4$ 
 are fixed.

- $(2\ 4)$ is a $2$-cycle (transposition): $2 \leftrightarrow 4$.

- $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4\ 5)$ is a $5$-cycle that moves every element.

content.title: Transposition
content.content:
A $2$-cycle $(a\ b)$ is called a **transposition**. 
It swaps $a$ and $b$ and fixes everything else.

content.title: Disjoint Cycles
content.content:
Two cycles are **disjoint** if they have no 
elements in common.

content.title: Disjoint Cycles Commute
content.content: If $\sigma$ and $\tau$ are disjoint cycles, then $\sigma\tau = \tau\sigma$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\sigma = (a_1\ \cdots\ a_k)$ and $\tau = (b_1\ \cdots\ b_m)$ with 
no $a_i$ equal to any $b_j$.

For any element $x$:

- If $x = a_i$, then $\sigma\tau(x) = \sigma(x) = a_{i+1}$ and 
 $\tau\sigma(x) = \tau(a_{i+1}) = a_{i+1}$.

- If $x = b_j$, similarly.

- If $x$ is in neither cycle, both sides equal $x$.
Thus $\sigma\tau = \tau\sigma$.

content.title: Cycle Decomposition Theorem
content.content:
Every permutation in $S_n$ can be written as a product of disjoint 
cycles. This decomposition is unique up to the order of the cycles.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding cycle decomposition
content.problem:
Let $\sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 & 1 & 6 & 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

Trace the orbits:

- $1 \to 3 \to 1$ gives $(1\ 3)$

- $2 \to 5 \to 2$ gives $(2\ 5)$

- $4 \to 6 \to 4$ gives $(4\ 6)$
Thus $\sigma = (1\ 3)(2\ 5)(4\ 6)$.

content.title: Order of a Permutation
content.content:
The order of a permutation is the least common multiple of the lengths 
of its disjoint cycles.
content.label: Theorem

items: Write $(1\ 2\ 3)(4\ 5)(1\ 4\ 3)$ as a product of disjoint cycles.
Find all permutations in $S_4$ that have order $4$.
What is the maximum order of any element of $S_7$?

Prove that every permutation can be written as a product of 
 transpositions.

marginNotes.text:
The wind blows to the south and turns 
to the north; round and round it goes, ever returning on its course.

marginNotes.author: Arthur Cayley
marginNotes.text:
Every permutation can be expressed as a 
product of disjoint cycles, unique up to order.
